summary:
The Cattle Period of Saharan rock art, dating approximately 4500–2500 BCE, represents one of the most prolific and artistically accomplished phases of prehistoric image-making in northern Africa. During this period, while the Sahara was still a savannah-like environment with lakes, rivers, and grasslands supporting large herds of cattle and wildlife, pastoral communities carved and painted thousands of images on rock surfaces across the central Saharan massifs — including Tassili n'Ajjer in Algeria, the Fezzan in Libya, and the Air mountains of Niger.

Cattle depictions dominate this phase, often shown with naturalistic rendering of markings and horns and sometimes accompanied by human herders. The images reflect a pastoral lifestyle in which cattle were central to social identity, ritual life, and economic survival. As [[event:1687|Saharan desiccation]] intensified after approximately 3000 BCE, these communities were forced to abandon many regions, migrating north to the Mediterranean coast, east to the Nile Valley (where they may have contributed to the development of [[event:1637|predynastic Egyptian]] cattle culture), or south into sub-Saharan Africa. The rock art they left behind constitutes one of the most significant archives of prehistoric human life on the continent.
